title: Measles
oneLiner: Highly contagious paramyxovirus causing fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and rash; preventable by MMR, can cause severe pneumonia and encephalitis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute laryngotracheobronchitis (croup) in young children:** Stridor, tachypnoea, intercostal recession, hypoxia; a "barking cough" + respiratory distress signals airway obstruction — this is not routine measles.
- **Measles pneumonitis (especially in immunocompromised):** Rapidly progressive hypoxia (SpO2 <90%), bilateral crackles, high fever; treat as medical emergency — not just “flu-like symptoms”.
- **Encephalitis:** Altered GCS (<13), seizures, focal neurology, headache; do not dismiss as fever-related confusion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Incubation:** 7–14 days (range 6–21)
- **Prodrome (2–4 days before rash):**
  - Fever > 38°C
  - Cough, coryza (runny nose), conjunctivitis (the “3 Cs”)
  - General malaise, anorexia, irritability
- **Koplik spots:** Small bluish-white spots on buccal mucosa (pathognomonic) — appear 1–2 days before rash
- **Rash:**
  - Maculopapular, erythematous, blanching
  - Starts behind ears → spreads to face → trunk → limbs (descending progression)
  - Lasts 4–7 days, fades in order of appearance
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Check for Koplik spots (tongue depressor + good light)
  - Note rash distribution and presence of fever
  - Lymphadenopathy (generalised)
  - Conjunctival injection (non-purulent)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Rubella (German measles):** Rash more fleeting, fever lower, Koplik spots absent, posterior auricular/occipital lymphadenopathy prominent
- **Scarlet fever:** Sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, circumoral pallor, high fever, tonsillitis/pharyngitis; no Koplik spots
- **Kawasaki disease:** Prolonged fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctival injection (non-purulent), red cracked lips, strawberry tongue, rash (polymorphous), cervical lymphadenopathy; no Koplik spots; check platelet count and echo for coronary aneurysms
- **Roseola infantum (HHV-6):** High fever that resolves when rash appears – rash after defervescence; child looks well between fever spikes; no Koplik spots
- **Enterovirus infections:** Hand-foot-mouth disease, herpangina; vesicular lesions, fever, no descending rash
- **Drug rash:** History of new drug exposure, no fever or prodrome, no Koplik spots
- **Splitter:** Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles – if present, diagnosis is clear; if absent but high clinical suspicion, proceed to testing.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Temperature (fever chart)
  - SpO2 on air – baseline
  - Clinical assessment of hydration (mucous membranes, skin turgor, urine output)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (lymphopenia common; neutrophilia suggests secondary infection)
  - U&E, creatinine (hydration)
  - CRP (elevated but non-specific)
  - Measles-specific IgM (acute infection – positive from day 3-4 of rash; may take 1-2 weeks to turn positive)
  - Measles IgG (to confirm past infection or vaccination; not for acute diagnosis)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR if respiratory distress, tachypnoea, or hypoxia – looking for consolidation or diffuse interstitial changes (measles pneumonitis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Throat swab or oral fluid for measles PCR (most sensitive + confirms genotype)
  - Urine PCR also possible
  - If encephalitis: CT head, LP (contraindicated if raised ICP), EEG – send CSF for measles PCR
- **Diagnostic criteria (clinical plus laboratory):**
  - WHO clinical case definition: fever >38°C + maculopapular rash + cough/coryza/conjunctivitis
  - Confirmed by detection of measles virus RNA (PCR) or IgM serology
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Measles PCR (throat swab/oral fluid) + serum IgM** – omission means failure to confirm diagnosis, delay public health notification, and miss opportunity to contact trace/protect vulnerable contacts.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Isolate immediately:** Single room with airborne precautions (negative pressure ideally, FFP3 mask for staff, gown, gloves). Sign door: “Measles – Airborne precautions”.
  - **Assess airway, breathing, circulation:** High-flow oxygen if SpO2 <92%.
  - **Supportive care:** Paracetamol 15 mg/kg (max 4g/day) or ibuprofen for fever (only if well-hydrated).
  - **Consider vitamin A:** For children with severe measles (pneumonia, diarrhoea, malnutrition). [Check local policy] – standard BNF dose: <6 months: 50,000 units; 6–12 months: 100,000 units; >12 months: 200,000 units orally once daily for 2 days. Third dose if signs of vitamin A deficiency (night blindness, Bitot spots).
  - **Notify local public health team** (Health Protection Team / CCDC) – does not wait for lab confirmation if clinical suspicion high.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue isolation until rash resolves (typically 4 days after onset) – in immunocompromised, isolate for duration of illness.
  - Monitor vital signs (NEWS2) 4-hourly.
  - Check SpO2 – if decreasing, repeat CXR.
  - Maintain hydration: oral fluids, IV if vomiting/dehydrated (0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid).
  - Treat secondary bacterial infections (e.g. otitis media, pneumonia) – use antibiotics per local guidelines (amoxicillin for otitis, co-amoxiclav for pneumonia).
  - Provide nutritional support – encourage high-calorie, high-protein intake.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated contacts: MMR within 72 hours of exposure; if immunocompromised or pregnant, give human normal immunoglobulin (HNIG) within 6 days.
  - Vaccination schedule: Ensure patient and siblings are up-to-date with MMR (can be given after recovery, but wait until out of acute illness).
  - Notify GP and immunisation service.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate (e.g. 30 mL/kg/day for paediatric, 2-3 L/day for adult) – adjust for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Only if prolonged immobility or additional risk factors – LMWH per local policy.
  - **Diet:** Normal as tolerated; no restrictions.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 4-hourly for stable, 1-2 hourly if febrile or respiratory concerns.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (supportive):**
  - Paracetamol oral/IV: Adult 15mg/kg (max 1g) 4-6 hourly (max 4g/24h); Child: 15mg/kg 4-6 hourly (max 60mg/kg/day)
  - Ibuprofen oral: Adult 200-400mg TDS (max 1.2g/day); Child: 5-10mg/kg TDS (max 30mg/kg/day) – avoid if dehydrated or renal impairment
- **Vitamin A:** For children with severe measles (see Management above). [Check local policy] for exact dose. BNF dose:
  - <6 months: 50,000 units orally once daily for 2 days
  - 6-12 months: 100,000 units once daily for 2 days
  - >12 months: 200,000 units once daily for 2 days; third dose if signs of deficiency
  - **Adults:** No standard dose – [check local policy]; consider 200,000 units if severe, but not routinely recommended.
- **Common alternatives:** None.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Vitamin A: hypervitaminosis A (rare if short course); caution in liver disease.
  - NSAIDs: contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, severe renal failure, asthma with NSAID sensitivity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: caution in severe hepatic impairment; max 2g/day if high risk.
  - Ibuprofen: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30-59.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vitamin A overdose** – can cause raised intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri), hepatotoxicity. Do not exceed 200,000 units per dose; do not repeat more than 2-3 days.
  - **Paracetamol overdose** – 4g daily is max; cumulative doses in anorexic patients can cause toxicity – track daily intake.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pneumonia (most common cause of death) – viral pneumonitis or secondary bacterial
  - Otitis media (up to 10% of children)
  - Laryngotracheobronchitis (croup) – can cause airway obstruction
  - Diarrhoea, dehydration
  - Febrile seizures
  - Encephalitis (1 in 1000) – features: altered consciousness, seizures, focal deficits
  - Myocarditis (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) – rare (1 in 100,000), fatal, appears 7-10 years later; dementia, myoclonus, ataxia
  - Can exacerbate pre-existing TB (tuberculosis)
sections.Prognosis:
- Self-limiting in 7-10 days in immunocompetent children; case fatality rate in UK <0.1% (higher in malnourished, immunocompromised, pregnant).
- Encephalitis carries 10-20% mortality and 30% neurological sequelae.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check vaccination history** – if patient (or sibling) missed MMR, document and arrange catch-up after recovery.
- **Notify public health immediately** – do not wait for PCR confirmation if clinical suspicion is high; contact tracing prevents outbreaks.
- **Give vitamin A to all children with severe measles** (pneumonia, diarrhoea, malnutrition) – reduces mortality and complications.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not discharge without confirming isolation** – patient with active rash is infectious from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset. Place in single room with airborne precautions.
- **Do not give MMR during acute illness** – wait until patient is well (at least 4 weeks post-recovery) to avoid vaccine failure.
- **Do not miss secondary bacterial infection** – rising NEWS, new focal consolidation on CXR, or purulent sputum should trigger antibiotics; don’t assume everything is viral.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Difficulty breathing, stridor, chest pain
  - Drowsiness, confusion, seizures
  - Inability to drink or signs of dehydration (reduced urine output, sunken eyes)
  - New high fever after initial defervescence (suggests secondary bacterial infection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72h if ongoing fever or respiratory symptoms
  - Audiology assessment if otitis media (booking note)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS <13 or new seizure
  - SpO2 <92% despite high-flow oxygen
  - Stridor or respiratory distress (need ENT/anaesthetics)
  - Suspected encephalitis (CT, LP, start acyclovir if HSV also possible)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising by 2 points
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Refractory hypoxia (PaO2/FiO2 <300)
  - Need for respiratory support (non-invasive or invasive ventilation)
  - Severe encephalitis with status epilepticus or raised ICP

rawMarkdown:
## Measles
**One-liner:** Highly contagious paramyxovirus causing fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and rash; preventable by MMR, can cause severe pneumonia and encephalitis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute laryngotracheobronchitis (croup) in young children:** Stridor, tachypnoea, intercostal recession, hypoxia; a "barking cough" + respiratory distress signals airway obstruction — this is not routine measles.
- **Measles pneumonitis (especially in immunocompromised):** Rapidly progressive hypoxia (SpO2 <90%), bilateral crackles, high fever; treat as medical emergency — not just “flu-like symptoms”.
- **Encephalitis:** Altered GCS (<13), seizures, focal neurology, headache; do not dismiss as fever-related confusion.

### Presentation
- **Incubation:** 7–14 days (range 6–21)
- **Prodrome (2–4 days before rash):**
  - Fever > 38°C
  - Cough, coryza (runny nose), conjunctivitis (the “3 Cs”)
  - General malaise, anorexia, irritability
- **Koplik spots:** Small bluish-white spots on buccal mucosa (pathognomonic) — appear 1–2 days before rash
- **Rash:**
  - Maculopapular, erythematous, blanching
  - Starts behind ears → spreads to face → trunk → limbs (descending progression)
  - Lasts 4–7 days, fades in order of appearance
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Check for Koplik spots (tongue depressor + good light)
  - Note rash distribution and presence of fever
  - Lymphadenopathy (generalised)
  - Conjunctival injection (non-purulent)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Rubella (German measles):** Rash more fleeting, fever lower, Koplik spots absent, posterior auricular/occipital lymphadenopathy prominent
- **Scarlet fever:** Sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, circumoral pallor, high fever, tonsillitis/pharyngitis; no Koplik spots
- **Kawasaki disease:** Prolonged fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctival injection (non-purulent), red cracked lips, strawberry tongue, rash (polymorphous), cervical lymphadenopathy; no Koplik spots; check platelet count and echo for coronary aneurysms
- **Roseola infantum (HHV-6):** High fever that resolves when rash appears – rash after defervescence; child looks well between fever spikes; no Koplik spots
- **Enterovirus infections:** Hand-foot-mouth disease, herpangina; vesicular lesions, fever, no descending rash
- **Drug rash:** History of new drug exposure, no fever or prodrome, no Koplik spots
- **Splitter:** Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles – if present, diagnosis is clear; if absent but high clinical suspicion, proceed to testing.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Temperature (fever chart)
  - SpO2 on air – baseline
  - Clinical assessment of hydration (mucous membranes, skin turgor, urine output)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (lymphopenia common; neutrophilia suggests secondary infection)
  - U&E, creatinine (hydration)
  - CRP (elevated but non-specific)
  - Measles-specific IgM (acute infection – positive from day 3-4 of rash; may take 1-2 weeks to turn positive)
  - Measles IgG (to confirm past infection or vaccination; not for acute diagnosis)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR if respiratory distress, tachypnoea, or hypoxia – looking for consolidation or diffuse interstitial changes (measles pneumonitis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Throat swab or oral fluid for measles PCR (most sensitive + confirms genotype)
  - Urine PCR also possible
  - If encephalitis: CT head, LP (contraindicated if raised ICP), EEG – send CSF for measles PCR
- **Diagnostic criteria (clinical plus laboratory):**
  - WHO clinical case definition: fever >38°C + maculopapular rash + cough/coryza/conjunctivitis
  - Confirmed by detection of measles virus RNA (PCR) or IgM serology
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Measles PCR (throat swab/oral fluid) + serum IgM** – omission means failure to confirm diagnosis, delay public health notification, and miss opportunity to contact trace/protect vulnerable contacts.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Isolate immediately:** Single room with airborne precautions (negative pressure ideally, FFP3 mask for staff, gown, gloves). Sign door: “Measles – Airborne precautions”.
  - **Assess airway, breathing, circulation:** High-flow oxygen if SpO2 <92%.
  - **Supportive care:** Paracetamol 15 mg/kg (max 4g/day) or ibuprofen for fever (only if well-hydrated).
  - **Consider vitamin A:** For children with severe measles (pneumonia, diarrhoea, malnutrition). [Check local policy] – standard BNF dose: <6 months: 50,000 units; 6–12 months: 100,000 units; >12 months: 200,000 units orally once daily for 2 days. Third dose if signs of vitamin A deficiency (night blindness, Bitot spots).
  - **Notify local public health team** (Health Protection Team / CCDC) – does not wait for lab confirmation if clinical suspicion high.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue isolation until rash resolves (typically 4 days after onset) – in immunocompromised, isolate for duration of illness.
  - Monitor vital signs (NEWS2) 4-hourly.
  - Check SpO2 – if decreasing, repeat CXR.
  - Maintain hydration: oral fluids, IV if vomiting/dehydrated (0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid).
  - Treat secondary bacterial infections (e.g. otitis media, pneumonia) – use antibiotics per local guidelines (amoxicillin for otitis, co-amoxiclav for pneumonia).
  - Provide nutritional support – encourage high-calorie, high-protein intake.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated contacts: MMR within 72 hours of exposure; if immunocompromised or pregnant, give human normal immunoglobulin (HNIG) within 6 days.
  - Vaccination schedule: Ensure patient and siblings are up-to-date with MMR (can be given after recovery, but wait until out of acute illness).
  - Notify GP and immunisation service.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate (e.g. 30 mL/kg/day for paediatric, 2-3 L/day for adult) – adjust for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Only if prolonged immobility or additional risk factors – LMWH per local policy.
  - **Diet:** Normal as tolerated; no restrictions.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 4-hourly for stable, 1-2 hourly if febrile or respiratory concerns.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (supportive):**
  - Paracetamol oral/IV: Adult 15mg/kg (max 1g) 4-6 hourly (max 4g/24h); Child: 15mg/kg 4-6 hourly (max 60mg/kg/day)
  - Ibuprofen oral: Adult 200-400mg TDS (max 1.2g/day); Child: 5-10mg/kg TDS (max 30mg/kg/day) – avoid if dehydrated or renal impairment
- **Vitamin A:** For children with severe measles (see Management above). [Check local policy] for exact dose. BNF dose:
  - <6 months: 50,000 units orally once daily for 2 days
  - 6-12 months: 100,000 units once daily for 2 days
  - >12 months: 200,000 units once daily for 2 days; third dose if signs of deficiency
  - **Adults:** No standard dose – [check local policy]; consider 200,000 units if severe, but not routinely recommended.
- **Common alternatives:** None.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Vitamin A: hypervitaminosis A (rare if short course); caution in liver disease.
  - NSAIDs: contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, severe renal failure, asthma with NSAID sensitivity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: caution in severe hepatic impairment; max 2g/day if high risk.
  - Ibuprofen: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30-59.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vitamin A overdose** – can cause raised intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri), hepatotoxicity. Do not exceed 200,000 units per dose; do not repeat more than 2-3 days.
  - **Paracetamol overdose** – 4g daily is max; cumulative doses in anorexic patients can cause toxicity – track daily intake.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pneumonia (most common cause of death) – viral pneumonitis or secondary bacterial
  - Otitis media (up to 10% of children)
  - Laryngotracheobronchitis (croup) – can cause airway obstruction
  - Diarrhoea, dehydration
  - Febrile seizures
  - Encephalitis (1 in 1000) – features: altered consciousness, seizures, focal deficits
  - Myocarditis (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) – rare (1 in 100,000), fatal, appears 7-10 years later; dementia, myoclonus, ataxia
  - Can exacerbate pre-existing TB (tuberculosis)

### Prognosis
- Self-limiting in 7-10 days in immunocompetent children; case fatality rate in UK <0.1% (higher in malnourished, immunocompromised, pregnant).
- Encephalitis carries 10-20% mortality and 30% neurological sequelae.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check vaccination history** – if patient (or sibling) missed MMR, document and arrange catch-up after recovery.
- **Notify public health immediately** – do not wait for PCR confirmation if clinical suspicion is high; contact tracing prevents outbreaks.
- **Give vitamin A to all children with severe measles** (pneumonia, diarrhoea, malnutrition) – reduces mortality and complications.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not discharge without confirming isolation** – patient with active rash is infectious from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset. Place in single room with airborne precautions.
- **Do not give MMR during acute illness** – wait until patient is well (at least 4 weeks post-recovery) to avoid vaccine failure.
- **Do not miss secondary bacterial infection** – rising NEWS, new focal consolidation on CXR, or purulent sputum should trigger antibiotics; don’t assume everything is viral.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Difficulty breathing, stridor, chest pain
  - Drowsiness, confusion, seizures
  - Inability to drink or signs of dehydration (reduced urine output, sunken eyes)
  - New high fever after initial defervescence (suggests secondary bacterial infection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72h if ongoing fever or respiratory symptoms
  - Audiology assessment if otitis media (booking note)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS <13 or new seizure
  - SpO2 <92% despite high-flow oxygen
  - Stridor or respiratory distress (need ENT/anaesthetics)
  - Suspected encephalitis (CT, LP, start acyclovir if HSV also possible)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising by 2 points
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Refractory hypoxia (PaO2/FiO2 <300)
  - Need for respiratory support (non-invasive or invasive ventilation)
  - Severe encephalitis with status epilepticus or raised ICP
